title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb deps to branch `main` — non-reproducible builds
description: Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"`, which is a mutable branch name. Move's dependency resolver re-fetches per build, so any upstream change (security fix, breaking rename, account/policy refactor) is silently pulled in. The Move.lock does pin to commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the lock only matters for the declared `testnet` env; a fresh checkout on `mainnet` or a future env would re-resolve against the branch.
impact: A future `sui move build` could produce a binary that transparently diverges from the audited commit — including behavioural changes in `policy::new_for_currency`, `account::deposit_balance`, or the `clawback_funds::resolve` semantics that Hadron relies on. The integration boundary is broken without warning.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the exact commit SHA in the lockfile (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) for both packages, and do the same for any additional environment (mainnet) before that environment is opened.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: pas_supply::seize blocked by mint/redeem pause — can strand compliance action
description: `seize` calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` at entry. Operationally, mint/redeem pause is a *commercial* lever (stop issuance) while seize is a *compliance* lever (regulator order, sanctions). Conflating the two means a treasurer who pauses issuance of a PAS asset also loses the ability to seize funds — the typical operational sequence when an incident is detected is the exact opposite (pause first, seize second).
impact: Compliance officer cannot execute a seize while mint/redeem is paused, delaying regulatory action. Workaround requires unpausing, seizing, repausing — widening the window where attackers can mint/redeem.
recommendation: Remove the `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` check from `seize`, or gate it behind a separate `assert_seize_enabled<T>()` flag that defaults to enabled and is controlled by a different permission.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer conflates mint/redeem pause with transfer pause
description: The transfer approval check uses `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>` as the transfer-pause gate. There is no independent `transfer_paused<T>` flag. Because transfers in a PAS system are usually the *last* operation to pause (you want to stop mint while letting existing holders unwind), this conflation forces an all-or-nothing pause that contradicts standard issuer playbooks.
impact: Treasury cannot freeze issuance without also freezing secondary transfers; operational granularity is lost. Leads #4 of the orchestrator pre-read flags this as a design choice to confirm — it should not be reused as-is.
recommendation: Introduce a `PauseTransferPermission` / independent `transfer_paused_key<T>()` DOF, or at minimum document that the PAS transfer gate is deliberately coupled to the mint/redeem gate so downstream operators do not assume orthogonality.

title: treasury::is_whitelisted/can_send/can_receive abort on non-PAS coins (opaque DOF error)
description: Public view helpers `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow` the whitelist DOF. When `T` is a non-PAS registered coin (or an unregistered coin), the DOF does not exist and the call aborts with a framework-level `EFieldDoesNotExist`, not a Hadron error. Off-chain tooling calling these as `devInspect` will receive an opaque abort code instead of a sensible `false`.
impact: Poor UX for off-chain callers. Also creates inconsistency: `is_coin_registered<T>` correctly returns `bool`, but the sibling whitelist queries abort. Indexers and compliance dashboards that query blindly across all known coin types will fail on PAS/non-PAS mismatches.
recommendation: Early-return `false` if `!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)`: `if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false;` at the top of each of the three view functions. Leave `assert_can_send` / `assert_can_receive` as abort-on-false.

title: pas_admin whitelist mutators accept non-PAS coin T — abort inside DOF
description: The four whitelist mutators (`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`) gate on `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`, which only asserts `is_coin_registered<T>()` inside `auth::new_auth`. A holder of `WhitelistManagePermission` can construct an Auth for a *regular* (non-PAS) coin and call these mutators; the call then proceeds into `treasury::add_to_whitelist<T>` which `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut`s a non-existent DOF, aborting with a framework error, not a Hadron error.
impact: Confusing error surface and a defence-in-depth gap: the type parameter T is not proven to be a PAS coin. While the actual state change is blocked, the error message is unhelpful and wastes gas. If the DOF layout ever changes (e.g. PolicyCap is added to `add_coin_caps` for some reason), the guard collapses.
recommendation: At the top of each treasury-side whitelist mutator, add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` mirroring the guard already present in `policy_cap<T>` (line 273).

title: register_pas_asset accepts unused `version: &Version` — misleading API
description: `register_pas_asset` takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. The only use is to pass into `register_transfer_template`, which also does not call `check_is_valid` — it only embeds `object::id(version)` into the PTB template. The version gate is implicitly relied upon via `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` construction (which does check version), but the signature implies a direct check that does not exist.
impact: Future refactors that no longer require a `TreasuryAuth` (e.g. an admin-cap bypass) would silently lose version enforcement. The dead argument also adds gas cost and review burden.
recommendation: Either (a) remove `version` from `register_pas_asset`'s parameter list entirely since it is consumed only as an object-id baked into the template — obtain the version shared-object ID another way — or (b) add an explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at the top. `update_transfer_template` has the same issue at L113–121.

title: treasury::policy_cap<T> getter is dead code
description: `policy_cap<T>(&Treasury)` is defined as `public(package)` but has zero call sites in the Hadron codebase. The PolicyCap is stored in `add_pas_coin_caps` but never retrieved — Hadron's entire clawback/transfer flow resolves via `pas::clawback_funds::resolve` and `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance`, which take a `&Policy<T>` shared object, not the PolicyCap.
impact: API surface bloat. If the getter is later called and the caller mutates the policy (e.g. `set_required_approval`), it would bypass Hadron's permission model since anyone with package access could call it. Removing it tightens the attack surface.
recommendation: Remove the `policy_cap<T>` function, or if it is meant to support a future admin operation (re-setting approvals), gate it behind `Auth<RegisterCoinPermission, T>` and document the intended use.

title: pas_supply::burn uses RedeemPermission — does not match conceptual 'burn/clawback'
description: `burn` is authorised by `Auth<RedeemPermission, T>`. Semantically the operation is a clawback-driven burn (funds are extracted via `ClawbackFunds`, not returned by the holder). Reusing RedeemPermission conflates the two: a role with `RedeemPermission` is intended for user-redemption flows (where a holder sends coin back to the treasury via `supply::redeem`), but for PAS it now also grants clawback-burn power without the holder's action. This is a silent privilege escalation when migrating roles between regulated and PAS assets.
impact: A role designed for a regulated coin's redeem window inherits destructive clawback rights when the same treasury later registers a PAS asset with the same type parameter in-scope. Separation-of-duties is weakened.
recommendation: Introduce a dedicated `PasBurnPermission` (or reuse `SeizePermission` since it is the same clawback mechanic) and require it in `pas_supply::burn`. Align with the spec's treatment of seize as a distinct compliance permission.

title: WhitelistManagePermission collapses spec's split add/remove permissions
description: The Notion spec for PAS modules splits whitelist authority into `WhitelistAddPermission` / `WhitelistRemovePermission` (and treats `set_whitelist_send/receive` as `WhitelistAddPermission`-gated). The code collapses these into a single `WhitelistManagePermission`, which is semantically broader.
impact: Operational risk: a role scoped 'add-only' in spec is necessarily clawback-capable in code. Audit findings and compliance documents that cite spec role names will not map 1:1 to on-chain roles. Documented as a known spec drift per §10 leads #1 of the context bundle.
recommendation: Either (a) split into two permission markers to match the spec, or (b) update the spec and the PR description to acknowledge the rename and justify the consolidation.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: WhitelistEntry lacks event for state change on add (defaults are implicit)
description: `add_to_whitelist` emits `WhitelistAdded<T>(addr)` but not the fact that `can_send=true, can_receive=true` is the default. Indexers that rely on the last `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` event will miss the initial state and show `false` until an explicit `set_whitelist_send` call fires. Similarly, `remove_from_whitelist` emits a generic removal event without the prior send/receive booleans, making reconstruction from the event stream lossy.
impact: Off-chain state reconstruction is incorrect without querying on-chain state directly. Subtle but adds non-trivial indexer complexity.
recommendation: Have `emit_whitelist_added` carry `{ addr, can_send, can_receive }` (defaulting to `true, true`), or emit both `WhitelistAdded` and the two initial `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events.

title: Template registration baked with Hadron package-id — upgrade path silently breaks
description: `register_transfer_template` computes the package ID via `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. This is the *current* Hadron package ID at registration time. After a Hadron package upgrade, the defining-ids type name still points at the original package (where `TransferApproval` was first defined), which is the intended stable address. However, if a future upgrade introduces a NEW approval witness or renames the entrypoint, the template will autoresolve into the *original* package's `approve_transfer` — not the upgraded one. Consumers who assume template autoresolve 'tracks the latest Hadron upgrade' will be surprised.
impact: After upgrade, SDK autoresolve may execute the *old* `approve_transfer` logic (which still works because original-id calls are routed to the latest version by the loader, but this is opaque to reviewers). If the original module is ever disabled via `version` gate, autoresolve breaks silently.
recommendation: Document that `update_transfer_template<T>` MUST be re-invoked after any Hadron upgrade that changes `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` semantics. Consider emitting a `TransferTemplateRegistered` event to give off-chain observers a breadcrumb for upgrade coordination. Also note this caveat in README/integration docs.

title: No Move tests for any new PAS module
description: `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, `pas_transfer.move` contain zero `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` functions. Treasury's new PAS helpers (`add_pas_coin_caps`, whitelist table management) are also untested. Given the multi-dependency nature (hadron ↔ pas ↔ ptb) and the criticality of whitelist enforcement, automated tests are essential for regression.
impact: Behavioural changes in upstream `pas::policy`, `pas::templates`, or `pas::account` will only be caught by integration testing on testnet. Version-gate regressions and permission escalations (see R9-005, R9-008) would slip through.
recommendation: Add at minimum: (a) a happy-path test that creates a treasury, registers a PAS asset, whitelists two addresses, mints into an account, transfers and seizes; (b) negative tests for `add_to_whitelist<RegularCoin>` (must abort with `ENotPasCoin` after R9-005 fix); (c) a test that verifies `supply::mint<PasCoin>` aborts with `EPasCoinNotAllowed`; (d) a test that exercises the `clawback_funds::resolve` boundary to confirm approval witness matching.

title: Move.toml edition `2024.beta` diverges from recommended `2024`
description: The root package declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. The stable `2024` edition has been available and is the recommended edition for production code. `2024.beta` may introduce non-final syntax/behaviour that shifts under the reviewer.
impact: Low — compilation works today. But may trigger deprecation warnings or require manifest updates with newer toolchains. Mixing with `token_template_pas/Move.toml` (which uses `edition = "2024"`) is also inconsistent.
recommendation: Update to `edition = "2024"` after confirming no beta-only features (e.g. unreleased macro syntax) are used.
evidence:
[package]
name = "hadron"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024.beta"

title: PAS token template bytecode hex is un-regeneratable (no generator script)
description: The 1.1 KB hex blob is a hand-pasted compilation artefact of `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. There is no comment, no script, and no CI check tying the hex to the .move source. If the template changes (e.g. add a new constant, change module structure), the script will silently deploy the STALE bytecode while the admin believes the new .move is in use.
impact: Drift between `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and the compiled artefact goes undetected. Patched constants (symbol, name, etc.) rely on exact byte offsets — a recompile can shift these, making `patchConstants` silently corrupt the bytecode. There is no integrity check.
recommendation: Add a build script (e.g. `scripts/build-pas-template-bytecode.ts`) that invokes `sui move build --path token_template_pas`, reads the produced `.mv` module, and emits the hex into `getBytecode.ts`. Add a CI step that runs it and fails on diff. Alternatively, read the bytecode from disk at runtime using `readFileSync` on the `.mv` output. Document the regeneration command in a comment block above the hex.

title: TS `pkg()` can return '' — silent wrong-package Move calls
description: `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` when the deploy-output file is missing or unreadable. Every PAS operation script (`registerPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, etc.) calls `pkg()` and passes the empty string directly into `package:` of Move calls. Downstream, `@mysten/sui/transactions` will either error obscurely or emit a call to the zero-package address.
impact: A misconfigured deploy-output results in an immediate tx failure that looks like a network or SDK problem, not a config problem. No early fail-fast.
recommendation: Make `pkg()` assert: `if (!id) throw new Error('Hadron package not configured — run deploy first')`, with the same guard at `CONSTANTS.versionObjectId` usage sites.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset derives coin type via substring matching on Sui type names
description: Object-ID extraction uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<`, `::coin_registry::Currency<`. If a future SDK version changes the fully-qualified type string format (e.g. adds whitespace, reorders, uses `TreasuryCapV2`), the matcher silently fails and the returned `treasuryCapObjectId` remains `''`. The later guard `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...)` catches zero-results but not *wrong* results.
impact: Fragile parsing tied to undocumented framework string format. If PAS token template package ever exports its own type with a similar tail (e.g. `MyTreasuryCap<T>`), the matcher could even pick the wrong object.
recommendation: Prefer parsing the structured `type` property of each object and comparing components (package = `0x2`, module = `coin`, name = `TreasuryCap`) rather than substring-match. The `@mysten/sui/utils` `parseStructTag` helper is the supported API.

title: mintPasTokens accepts JS `number` for u64 amount — overflow at 2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(.. , amount: number)` uses JavaScript `number` for a u64 Move parameter. Above 2^53, precision is lost silently. With 6 decimals, 2^53 represents 9,007,199,254 'whole units' — plausible for institutional bond notionals. The downstream BCS serialization treats it as a u64 but the JS value has already been coerced.
impact: Large mint amounts silently round. No runtime guard.
recommendation: Accept `bigint | number`, pass through `BigInt(amount)`, and reject `number` values above `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` with a thrown error. The `mintTokens` regulated-coin operation likely has the same issue and should be fixed in lockstep.

title: full-setup.ts leaks SIGNER_1_PK env without guard
description: `const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;` uses non-null assertion and will produce an obscure crash (`fromBase64` on undefined) when the env var is missing. No validation that it is base64 of an Ed25519 pubkey of the expected length.
impact: Example script — low blast radius, but copy-pasted into production onboarding scripts could hide a misconfiguration.
recommendation: Replace `!` with an explicit check: `if (!process.env.SIGNER_1_PK) throw new Error('SIGNER_1_PK required for full-setup')`. Validate the decoded length equals Ed25519 pubkey length + flag byte (33 bytes).
evidence:
const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;
    const receiver = new Ed25519PublicKey(fromBase64(pk1B64).slice(1)).toSuiAddress();

title: pas_supply::mint does not version-check — relies on Auth construction
description: `mint`, `burn`, `seize` do not call `version.check_is_valid()` directly. They rely on the fact that `Auth<P, T>` construction in `auth::new_auth` performed the check. This is fine for a single-PTB flow, but if an Auth is constructed and a subsequent migrate call blocks the old version in the same PTB, the downstream mint still proceeds because Auth has `drop` and the caller holds a reference.
impact: Theoretical — requires a malicious PTB author to race migrate + mint. Low likelihood because version migrate requires DeployerCap. Still, the normal pattern across the codebase is to re-check version at every public entrypoint.
recommendation: Either (a) take `version: &Version` and call `check_is_valid()` at entry, mirroring `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, or (b) document the rationale for relying on Auth-time validation in a module-level comment.

title: pas_supply::mint emits `recipient = account.owner()` — misleading for UID-owned accounts
description: `account.owner()` returns a wallet OR a `uid.to_inner().to_address()` (the synthetic address of an object-owned account per `pas::account::new_auth_as_object`). The `PasMinted<T>` event stores this address as `recipient`, and the whitelist was presumably populated with the same synthetic address. Off-chain observers interpreting the event as a wallet address will mislabel object-owned flows.
impact: Misleading off-chain data. Compliance dashboards may flag or misattribute UID-owned balances.
recommendation: Also include `account_id: ID` (the PAS account object ID) in `PasMinted<T>` so indexers can disambiguate. The deposit itself lives at `object::id(account)`, so the account ID is the source of truth.

title: Whitelist addition on non-whitelisted-before address has no initial-state event fidelity
description: `WhitelistAdded<T>` carries only `addr`. `WhitelistRemoved<T>` is identical. The `WhitelistSendUpdated<T>`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated<T>` events carry `enabled`. Consumers reconstructing state from events cannot tell the difference between 'addr was added and defaulted can_send=true' and 'addr was added and never had send toggled'. (Related to R9-010 above.)
impact: Low — off-chain indexers can query on-chain state. But event-sourced architectures take a hit.
recommendation: Fold the initial booleans into `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr, can_send, can_receive }`. Leaves the other two events unchanged.

title: approve_transfer does not emit an event when request is approved but resolve_balance fails
description: `approve_transfer` emits `PasTransferApproved<T>` at the end, after `request.approve(...)`. But the actual balance move happens later in `send_funds::resolve_balance` (called by the SDK autoresolve, not by this function). If a PTB contains both `approve_transfer` and `resolve_balance` and the latter reverts for reasons outside Hadron's control (policy versioning, approval mismatch), the event was emitted but the transfer did not occur — except the whole PTB reverts atomically, so the event is also rolled back. OK.
impact: None — atomicity protects the invariant. Flagging as info because the inverse (emit after resolve) is structurally cleaner but cannot be done from this module since resolve happens upstream.
recommendation: Document in a comment that `PasTransferApproved<T>` only fires if the full PTB (including `send_funds::resolve_balance`) commits, so event-sourced indexers can treat it as authoritative.

title: treasury error code ENotPasCoin defined but underused
description: `ENotPasCoin` is declared but used only in `policy_cap<T>` (L273). All whitelist helpers and mutators (which should also check `is_pas_coin<T>`) rely on opaque framework aborts instead. See R9-004 and R9-005 for the correctness impact.
impact: Inconsistent error surface; code's declared errors don't match observed runtime aborts.
recommendation: Use `ENotPasCoin` in `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive` (via early-return with false), and in `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` (as an explicit assert).
evidence:
#[error]
const ENotPasCoin: vector<u8> = b"Coin type is not registered as a PAS asset";

title: token_template_pas Move.toml missing name comment / package identity
description: The template's Move.toml uses `edition = "2024"` (stable, good) but has no `version`, no git-tag, and no comment pointing at `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/getBytecode.ts`. A reviewer coming to this file has no way to know it is exclusively consumed as a WASM-patched blob.
impact: Documentation gap only.
recommendation: Add a top-of-file comment in `token_template.move` noting: (1) this package is published per-asset via patchConstants, (2) the OTW name is rewritten via `update_identifiers`, (3) the bytecode hex lives at `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/getBytecode.ts`.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

title: WhitelistKey doc comment is wrong (Table<address, bool> vs WhitelistEntry)
description: The doc comment says 'DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets)'. The actual table stored in `add_pas_coin_caps` is `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` where `WhitelistEntry` is `{ can_send: bool, can_receive: bool }`.
impact: Incorrect docs. A developer trying to borrow the table with the wrong value type will get a type error, but the first time they read the comment they'll be confused.
recommendation: Update the comment to `DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets)`.
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: pas_admin::register_transfer_template uses byte-string literals over a `const`
description: The module and function names (`"pas_transfer"`, `"approve_transfer"`) are embedded as byte-string literals inline. If the module is ever renamed, the rename will succeed silently and the PTB template will reference the old name.
impact: Refactor hazard.
recommendation: Extract `PAS_TRANSFER_MODULE: vector<u8> = b"pas_transfer";` and `APPROVE_TRANSFER_FN: vector<u8> = b"approve_transfer";` as module-level consts.
evidence:
let cmd = ptb::move_call(
        approval_type.address_string().to_string(),
        b"pas_transfer".to_string(),
        b"approve_transfer".to_string(),

title: pas_supply.move duplicates EMintAmountZero error with supply.move
description: `pas_supply::EMintAmountZero` duplicates `supply::EMintAmountZero` verbatim. Since the two modules now coexist and operate on disjoint coin sets (enforced by `EPasCoinNotAllowed`), consolidation would reduce duplication.
impact: Low — same abort code value means same error behaviour.
recommendation: Consider lifting shared amount-zero errors into a `hadron::errors` module, or accept the duplication and silence the code review.

title: Request resolve contract: upstream uses `zip_do_ref` over VecSet ordering — witness set must match exactly
description: Upstream `pas::request::resolve` (at /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/request.move L44–52) uses `VecSet.into_keys().zip_do_ref(...)`, which compares approvals and required_approvals in insertion order. `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` sets exactly one required approval per action (`TransferApproval<T>` for send_funds, `ClawbackApproval<T>` for clawback_funds) — so there is only one slot and zip-do-ref is trivially satisfied. If Hadron ever adds a second approval to either action, the insertion order on Hadron's side MUST match the order on the policy's side, or `EInsufficientApprovals` will abort.
impact: Future hazard: second-approval additions will produce hard-to-debug `EInsufficientApprovals` aborts if insertion order diverges.
recommendation: When adding multi-witness actions, document the canonical ordering and add a Move test that asserts the observed ordering matches expectations. Alternatively, push upstream PAS to compare by set-equality instead of zip.

title: approve_transfer uses `&Treasury` — OK, but check ordering emits event after approve
description: Order: version check → read sender/recipient/amount → amount>0 → pause check → send/receive checks → approve → emit. Good ordering: approve is last-but-one. If any assert after version check aborts, the request is not mutated (no approval added). No silent state leakage.
impact: None — documenting positive behaviour.
recommendation: Consider adding a comment that explicitly marks `approve` as the state-committing point.

title: pas.ts registerPasAsset does not guard against 'already registered'
description: The script does not query on-chain state before attempting `registerPasAsset`. If the coin type is already registered as PAS (second run of the full-setup example), the tx will abort with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`, consuming gas and emitting a failure log with no remediation hint.
impact: Operational ergonomics only — no correctness impact. But first-time integrators will see a confusing failure.
recommendation: Before calling `registerPasAsset`, dev-inspect `treasury::is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)` and short-circuit with a clear message if already registered.
